text: The patient is positioned in the supine lithotomy position as the surgical robot is docked over the left abdomen. A five millimeter optical trocar is inserted through a right upper quadrant incision to gain intraperitoneal access, utilizing a four trocar technique for the robotic colostomy reversal.

The initial laparoscopic evaluation shows minimal adhesions. A 12mm balloon trocar is placed in the right abdomen for the camera, a 15mm trocar is positioned in the epigastrium for the endo-wrist straight blur, and an 8mm trocar is inserted in the right lower quadrant.
The surgeon evaluates the length of the distal colon and performs lysis of omental adhesions using a vessel sealer. The proximal colon is mobilized to ensure adequate length for the procedure.

The surgeon uses the endo-wrist stapler to grasp the proposed plane of transection on the proximal colon, visible in the top left corner of the screen. The blade is activated by depressing the yellow pedal on the surgery console, completing the transection with another fire.

The surgeon uses an epiploic appendage of the distal colon segment to secure it to the sidewall, ensuring adequate laxity of the proximal colon for tension-free re-anastomosis. A 3-0 silk suture is employed to approximate the proximal and distal colon segments.

The surgeon creates a colotomy in the distal segment, exposing the colonic mucosa. Subsequently, a colotomy is made in the proximal segment. The blades of the stapler are then inserted through the colotomy sites in both the proximal and distal segments.
The visual cues on the upper left corner of the screen indicate the readiness of the stapler to fire. The surgeon closes the common channel using a running 3-0 PDS suture.

The surgeon reinforces the closure with interrupted three-oh PDS sutures. Anesthesia injects five cc's of indocyanine green, revealing well-vascularized proximal and distal colon within a minute. The top of the suture line is over-sewn with a black silk suture.
